reasoning: Butterflies are rigid in the sense that an instance cannot cease to be a butterfly without losing its identity (it doesn't change into another kind of animal). It provides its own identity conditions for its instances.

Color properties like 'Red' are rigid, as they cannot change without losing their identity (it is always the same color). It carries identity conditions (i.g., an object is red if and only if it cannot be another color) but does not supply its own. It is anti-unity as it cannot be a single type of entity by itself, and it is dependent on a specific external entity (the object or surface that is being colored).

Food is rigid because an instance of a food type cannot cease to be that type without losing its identity (it doesn't change into another type). It carries identity conditions inherited from its parent types but does not supply a unique identity condition of its own.

Animal is a rigid property, as an instance cannot cease to be an animal without losing its identity (it doesn't change into another kind). It provides its own identity conditions for its instances and is a single, unifying concept.

RedApple is a rigid property, as it cannot change without losing its identity (it is not the same as an apple that is not red). It supplies its own identity conditions for distinguishing it from other apples.

A Group is anti-rigid because its members can change, it carries identity but does not supply its own, it is non-unifying as it does not represent a single single entity, and it is dependent on its constituent members.

Country is a rigid property because its identity (e.g., France) remains the same over time. It supplies its own identity conditions (defined by political boundaries, history etc.). It is a single, discrete entity (unity) and exists independently of other entities.

According to the definition of AmountOfMatter, it is a rigid property because it cannot change without losing its identity (e.g., 1 gram of matter cannot cease to be 1 gram without losing its identity). It carries an identity condition (IC) based on the specific quantity, but does not supply its own IC (it is dependent on a higher-level entity or concept to supply the overall context). It is externally dependent on a specific matter, but the property itself is not inherently dependent on a specific matter to exist.

PhysicalObject is a rigid property, as it cannot change without losing its identity. It carries an identity condition (I) based on its unique physical properties, and supplies its own identity condition (O). It is a unifying concept, as it includes the various physical entities that share common characteristics. It is independent (D) as it does not depend on other entities for its basic existence.

GroupOfPeople is anti-rigid (~R) because its members can change over time without losing its identity (I). It does not provide its own identity conditions (-O) but rather relies on its members. It is externally dependent (+D) because the existence of a GroupOfPeople relies on the existence of its constituent people.

LivingBeing is anti-rigid (~R) because an individual living being can change and develop over time without losing its identity (I). It does not supply its own identity conditions (-O) but rather gets its identity from its anti-unifying nature (-U) and dependence (+D) on the environment and its internal processes.

SocialEntity is anti-rigid (~R) because its members and boundaries can change over time. It carries identity conditions (+I) inherited from its constituent individuals. It supplies its own identity conditions (+O) based on a shared goal or purpose. It is anti-unity (~U) because it is an aggregate or collection of distinct individuals that may not necessarily share a single organizing principle. It is dependent (+D) on the existence of the individuals that constitute it.

Location is rigid because a spatial or conceptual coordinate cannot cease to be a location without losing its nature. It carries identity conditions (e.g., spatial coordinates) and does not supply its own, as it applies to other entities (e.g., a person is located at a specific location). It is anti-unifying because it does not represent a single, discrete entity, but rather a relationship between a subject and a space. It is independent as it represents a space or position that does not depend on a specific subject.

It is a natural kind with a specific identity (it is not a role or a mixin). It is non-unifying as it is a single organism that does not supply identity to other entities.

Person is a rigid property, as an individual person cannot cease to exist without losing its identity (I) and being replaced by another. It supplies its own identity conditions (O) and is a single, discrete entity (U). It is not dependent on other external entities for its basic existence (-D).

LegalAgent is anti-rigid (~R) because the same entity can cease to be a legal agent without ceasing to exist. It is dependent (+D) because the status of being a legal agent depends on external factors like laws and regulations.

The term 'Entity' is anti-rigid because its identity (and its instances) depend on the specific context. It carries identity conditions (+I) inherited from the higher-level concept it applies to, but does not supply its own (+O). It is externally dependent (+D) on the specific entity it applies to.

Organizations are anti-rigid as their members and structure can change over time. However, they do not supply their own identity conditions, as they are defined by a specific purpose or goal. They are non-unifying as they are composed of distinct individuals or departments that may not share a common identity. They are dependent on a social or legal framework for their existence.

Agent is anti-rigid (~R) because an entity can become an agent or cease to be one without losing its identity (I). It does not supply its own identity conditions (-O) as it applies to a specific entity (e.g., Person Agent) that does supply its own. It is non-unifying (-U) because it does not create a single type of entity, but rather applies to diverse entities (e.g., Person Agent, Agent Agent) that already exist. It is dependent (+D) because the existence of an agent depends on a specific entity being able to perform actions.
